perturbation.original_text: IBM will perform the Services described in Attachment A and other applicable Attachments.
perturbation.changed_text: IBM will endeavor to perform the Services described in Attachment A and other applicable Attachments.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states a clear obligation. The modified text introduces 'endeavor,' which is vague and weakens the obligation. It suggests an attempt to perform without guaranteeing actual performance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Enforceability of Obligations
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law requires parties to fulfill their contractual obligations in good faith. 'Endeavor' introduces ambiguity, potentially allowing IBM to avoid performing specific services, thus violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing required for enforceable contracts under New York law.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract up PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract next SECTION 2-302 Unconscionable Contract or Clause This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-301 General Obligations of Parties Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 3 Section 2--301. General Obligations of Parties. The obligation of the seller is to transfer and deliver and that of the buyer is to accept and pay in accordance with the contract.
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'will perform' with 'will endeavor to perform,' introducing ambiguity regarding IBM's commitment. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that 'endeavor' weakens the obligation, suggesting an attempt without guaranteeing performance. The Law Explanation argues this violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under New York law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-301, states the general obligations of parties, emphasizing that the seller's obligation is to 'transfer and deliver...in accordance with the contract.' By using 'endeavor,' the contract arguably permits IBM to not fully 'transfer and deliver' the services, which contradicts the principle of fulfilling contractual obligations in good faith, leading to a legal contradiction as defined by the 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Customer will pay to IBM all applicable charges specified in Attachment C.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer will remit what they consider to be the applicable charges, based on their internal assessment, as possibly specified in Attachment C.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a clear payment obligation. The modified text introduces subjectivity, allowing the customer to pay based on their assessment, which is ambiguous and contradicts the intent of the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the N.Y. UCC states that if a price is not settled, the price is a reasonable price at the time of delivery. Giving the customer complete authority to determine payment is not in accordance with the law.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract previous SECTION 2-304 Price Payable in Money, Goods, Realty, or Otherwise up PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract next SECTION 2-306 Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-305 Open Price Term Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 3 Section 2--305. Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith. (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract. In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity by allowing the customer to pay based on their internal assessment, potentially deviating from the 'applicable charges specified in Attachment C'. This contradicts the principle outlined in NY UCC Section 2-305, which states that if a price is not settled, a reasonable price at the time of delivery should be considered. The modified text undermines this by giving the customer subjective authority, rather than adhering to a reasonable or agreed-upon price, thereby creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: IBM will implement the security options specified herein.
perturbation.changed_text: IBM will generally attempt to implement security options with features that resemble those specified herein.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a clear security implementation obligation. The modified text introduces vagueness, changing it from a mandatory implementation to a general attempt, weakening IBM's responsibility.
perturbation.law_explanation: This law requires businesses to implement and maintain reasonable security measures to protect private information. By only 'attempting' to implement security options, IBM may not be fulfilling its duty to provide reasonable security measures, contradicting this New York law.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates implementation of security options, while the modified text only requires a general attempt at implementing them. This introduces ambiguity and weakens IBM's obligation. The law explanation states that businesses are required to implement and maintain reasonable security measures to protect private information, and that by only 'attempting' to implement security options, IBM may not be fulfilling its duty to provide reasonable security measures, contradicting New York law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, under SECTION 899-BB Data security protections, implies a mandatory obligation for data security protections, which the modified text undermines.
